content: Anthropogenic emissions of greenhouse gases (GHGs) perturb Earth’s radiative equilibrium by enhancing the atmospheric concentration of infrared-absorbing molecules—primarily CO₂, CH₄, and N₂O—thereby increasing radiative forcing. These gases absorb terrestrial longwave radiation emitted by the surface and re-radiate energy isotropically, effectively trapping heat within the lower atmosphere. The spectral absorption overlaps and pressure broadening phenomena modulate the efficiency of this process, with CO₂’s well-mixed background and long atmospheric lifetime exerting a persistent forcing. This enhanced greenhouse effect reduces outgoing longwave radiation, inducing a positive radiative imbalance. Subsequent feedbacks—most notably water vapor amplification and cryosphere albedo changes—intensify the initial forcing, accelerating surface temperature rise. The net result is a sustained energy imbalance driving global warming, as confirmed by satellite measurements and surface temperature records. This mechanistic understanding, grounded in radiative transfer physics and corroborated by climate models, establishes greenhouse gas accumulation as the primary driver of contemporary climate change.
